title: The Role of Randomness in Stability

reviews.summary: This paper studies the intrinsic role of internal randomness in achieving strong stability guarantees in learning, specifically replicability and differential privacy (DP). The central contributions are (i) a tight weak-to-strong boosting theorem equating the number of random bits needed for better-than-1/2 replicability (certificate complexity) with the log-inverse of the best deterministic collision probability (global stability), up to a single bit; (ii) two-way reductions between differential privacy randomness complexity and global stability, including a cleaner user-level DP formulation; and (iii) an application to agnostic PAC learning showing that bounded randomness complexity is possible if and only if the Littlestone dimension is finite, with certificate complexity scaling as poly(d) + O(VC(H) log(1/α)) and sample complexity polynomial in 1/α (with an exponential-in-d factor), thereby resolving an open question on error-dependent (list-)replicability.
strengths: Theorem 2.1 gives a strikingly tight equivalence C_Glob ≤ C_Rep ≤ C_Glob + 1 and a principled amplification to ρ-replicability with an additive log(1/ρ) term, cleanly quantifying the “randomness budget” needed to strengthen deterministic stability into shared-coin replicability.

The DP results (Theorems 2.2 and 2.3) provide the first general, randomness-efficient two-way bridge between DP complexity and global stability, carefully controlling support size and leveraging distributional approximation to reduce randomness.

The agnostic PAC result connects randomness complexity to the Littlestone dimension, extending realizable-regime stability guarantees to the full agnostic setting with explicit dependence on α and identifying the right structural condition (finite Ldim) for bounded randomness complexity.
Solid theoretical development with detailed proof roadmaps and careful parameter accounting, including discussions of sample/computation/randomness tradeoffs and where blowups occur.
Constructions are modular and build on known primitives (heavy-hitter extraction, DP selection, perfect generalization), with clear justifications for each reduction step.
The paper is well-organized, motivating each definition (global stability variants, certificate complexity, DP complexity) and systematically comparing parameterized and parameter-free forms.
Technical overviews in Section 4 make the core ideas accessible, including helpful diagrams (e.g., the thresholding/rounding scheme for replicability).
Establishes a crisp foundational relationship between stability and randomness that unifies recent lines on replicability, DP complexity, and perfect generalization.
Resolves an open problem on agnostic error-dependent stability/replicability and frames a roadmap for designing randomness-efficient private/replicable learners.
Likely to influence both the theory of private/replicable learning and practical thinking about randomness budgets in privacy-preserving data analysis.
weaknesses: The DP-to-stability direction requires ε ≲ 1/√(T log T) and δ ≲ 1/T; while motivated via perfect generalization and often achievable, this excludes looser DP regimes and leaves open whether the dependence is inherent or an artifact of current reductions.

The agnostic PAC construction incurs exponential dependence on the Littlestone dimension in sample complexity (though improved in the heavy-hitter variant), limiting immediate practicality for high-d classes.
Some steps rely on perfect generalization as an intermediate notion; alternative pathways that bypass PG might yield cleaner or broader-parameter DP reductions and could improve constants.

Computational efficiency is not a focus; several procedures (e.g., enumerating labelings, list pruning) may be computationally heavy, and the paper does not analyze runtime or implementability in large-scale settings.
The main text states the DP results informally (Theorems 2.2–2.3); given their centrality, including formal statements (with explicit parameter ranges) in the main body would aid readers.
Some notation toggles between collision-based and heavy-hitter global stability; although Appendix B proves equivalence, restating the key equivalence in the main text would reduce cognitive load.

While the paper discusses related work broadly, a more explicit comparison of quantitative constants and randomness budgets with (Dixon et al., ICML’24) and (Canonne et al., ITCS’24) on canonical low-dimensional tasks would help situate the tightness of the new bounds.

The discussion of the independent concurrent work (Blonda et al., 2025) is helpful; a crisper side-by-side for the agnostic setting (assumptions, list vs heavy-hitter vs collision, dependence on VC(H) and α) would clarify the exact novelty frontier.
reviews.questions:
1. Theorem 2.1’s +1 additive gap: Do you have examples achieving C_Rep = C_Glob + 1 (i.e., showing the bound is tight up to constants), or can the +1 be removed with a refined construction?
2. Can the DP-to-stability reverse direction be proved without going through perfect generalization, potentially relaxing the ε ≲ 1/√(T log T) and δ ≲ 1/T requirements? Are there barriers (e.g., counterexamples) to such a direct route?
3. For the agnostic PAC learner, is the VC(H)·log(1/α) dependence in certificate complexity necessary? Can it be reduced to polylog(1/α) or made independent of VC(H) under additional assumptions (e.g., proper vs improper learning)?
4. What are the computational complexities (time/memory) of your constructions in Theorems 2.1–2.4? Are there polynomial-time instantiations for common classes (e.g., large-margin halfspaces, thresholds) with your stated randomness/sample bounds?
5. In Section 4.2 you rely on distributional approximation to reduce randomness (support-T distributions approximated within δ/2 using log T + log(2/δ) bits). Could you include a self-contained lemma or more details on this approximation step and how it composes with DP guarantees?
6. The discussion notes that subsampling-based DP amplification consumes Θ(T log(1/ε)) random bits. Do you see a plausible path to derandomize subsampling (e.g., via almost k-wise independence or PRGs) while preserving the statistical and privacy guarantees at O(log(T/ε)) randomness?
7. For the “heavy-hitter” improved sample complexity variant in the agnostic setting, could you include a formal theorem statement and proof sketch in the main text to highlight the improved dependence on d and β?
reviews.assessment:
This is a strong theoretical paper that makes clear, conceptually clean, and technically robust advances on a fundamental question: how much internal randomness is necessary for strong stability guarantees in learning? The tight equivalence between global stability and certificate complexity for replicability is especially elegant and will be a useful design and analysis tool. The two-way bridges to DP complexity, while parameter-laden, are carefully justified, and the user-level DP reformulation usefully distills the message into a parameter-free form. The agnostic PAC application fills a notable gap, tying bounded randomness complexity to finite Littlestone dimension with interpretable α-dependence and offering the first such bounds for important hypothesis classes.

The main limitations are quantitative rather than qualitative: reliance on PG in the DP reductions (hence relatively stringent ε, δ regimes), and exponential dependence on Ldim in agnostic learning sample complexity. Nonetheless, these are understandable given the current state of the art, the paper is transparent about them, and the results open concrete avenues for tightening. The writing is clear, the connections to related work are thoughtful, and the contributions should be of high interest to the ICML community working on stability, privacy, and reproducible learning. I recommend acceptance.
